source_paper_title: A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Nishimura_2024, title={A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0351}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0351}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Nishimura, Hiroki and Ok, Efe A.}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2249–2270} }
context:
Section 4, page 25 (end of Section 4, after Table 1)

In passing, we note that as an immediate consequence of Theorem 4.1, we have \(\mathrm{diam}_D(A(X)) \ge \mathrm{diam}_D(P(X)) \ge n2^{n-1} + 2 - 2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} - 2^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}.\) We do not presently know whether or not either of these inequalities hold as equalities.
title: Determine diameter of acyclic and preorder preference spaces under top-difference semimetric
background_latex:
Let \(X\) be a finite set of alternatives with \(|X|=n\ge 2\). An (acyclic) preference relation in the paper is modeled as an \emph{acyclic order} \(\%\) on \(X\), i.e., a reflexive binary relation whose strict (asymmetric) part \(\succ\) contains no directed cycle: there do not exist pairwise distinct \(z_1,\dots,z_k\in X\) with \(z_1\succ z_2\succ\cdots\succ z_k\succ z_1\).

For any binary relation \(R\) on \(X\) and any nonempty subset \(S\subseteq X\), the set of \(R\)-maximal elements of \(S\) is
\[M(S,R):=\{x\in S: \text{there is no }y\in S\text{ with }y\,R^{>}\,x\},\]
where \(R^{>}\) denotes the asymmetric part of \(R\). For acyclic orders \(\%\), every nonempty finite \(S\) has \(M(S,\%)\neq\varnothing\).

Define the \emph{top-difference semimetric} \(D\) on the set \(A(X)\) of all acyclic orders on \(X\) by
\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|,\]
where \(\triangle\) is symmetric difference and \(|\cdot|\) is cardinality. (Singleton and empty \(S\) contribute \(0\), but the definition sums over all \(S\subseteq X\).)

For any subset \(\mathcal{C}\subseteq A(X)\), its diameter with respect to \(D\) is
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(\mathcal{C}) := \max\{ D(\%,\mathcal{D}) : \%,\mathcal{D}\in \mathcal{C}\},\]
which is well-defined since \(\mathcal{C}\) is finite when \(X\) is finite.

Let \(P(X)\subseteq A(X)\) denote the set of all preorders on \(X\) (reflexive and transitive relations).
problem_definition_latex:
Compute (or give an exact closed-form expression for)
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\quad\text{and/or}\quad \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\]
as functions of \(n=|X|\), where \(D\) is the top-difference semimetric
\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|\]
on acyclic orders \(\%,\mathcal{D}\in A(X)\), and \(P(X)\) denotes the class of preorders on \(X\).

\[\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\quad\text{and/or}\quad \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\]
as functions of \(n=|X|\), where \(D\) is the top-difference semimetric

open_problem_quote: In passing, we note that as an immediate consequence of Theorem 4.1, we have \(\mathrm{diam}_D(A(X)) \ge \mathrm{diam}_D(P(X)) \ge n2^{n-1} + 2 - 2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} - 2^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}.\) We do not presently know whether or not either of these inequalities hold as equalities.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The quantities are finite maxima over finite (though huge) spaces; for small n one can brute-force or use MILP/DP to compute exact diameters and look for extremal pairs, providing data to refute conjectured equalities or suggest tight formulas.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate extremal constructions (pairs of relations) can be represented explicitly (e.g., adjacency matrices/DAGs), and the fitness signal is exactly computable via the polynomial-time formula for D given in the paper; evolutionary search can explore structures to maximize D for given n and hypothesize patterns.
